As a result, Delhi came under the control of British East India Company, and became a part of the North-Western Provinces. The Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II remained a mere figurehead.

question: where does the water come from to fill lake eyre
pred_answer: Lake Eyre is filled primarily by rivers flowing from the north-east part of the Lake Eyre Basin—especially from south-west and central Queensland—during the rainy season; major contributing rivers include the Neales, Macumba, Warburton, Stuart Creek, and Warriner catchments. Heavy local rainfall in those catchments can also directly fill parts of the lake.

citations.excerpt: During the rainy season, rivers from the north-east part of the Lake Eyre Basin—in outback (south-west and central) Queensland—flow towards the lake through the Channel Country.The amount of water from the monsoon determ...
citations.text: During the rainy season, rivers from the north-east part of the Lake Eyre Basin—in outback (south-west and central) Queensland—flow towards the lake through the Channel Country.The amount of water from the monsoon determines whether water will reach the lake and, if it does, how deep the lake will get.The average rainfall in the area of the lake is 100 to 150 millimetres (3.9 to 5.9 in) per year.
citations.critique_reason: The chunk explains the source of water for Lake Eyre, mentioning rivers from the north-east and the role of the monsoon.

citations.critique_reason: The chunk mentions rivers that contributed water to Lake Eyre, but it does not fully explain the overall source of water for the lake in general.

citations.excerpt: The Lake Eyre Basin is a large endorheic (internally draining) system surrounding the lakebed, the lowest part of which is filled with the characteristic salt pan caused by the seasonal expansion and subsequent evaporati...
citations.text: The Lake Eyre Basin is a large endorheic (internally draining) system surrounding the lakebed, the lowest part of which is filled with the characteristic salt pan caused by the seasonal expansion and subsequent evaporation of the trapped waters.
citations.critique_reason: The chunk mentions the Lake Eyre Basin and that water is trapped and evaporates, implying a source, but does not explicitly state where the water comes from.

question: how did early humans make use of stones during the prehistoric period

question: what does the pearl symbolize in the bible
pred_answer: In the Bible, the pearl symbolizes the great value of the Kingdom of Heaven (Matthew 13:45–46, Parable of the Pearl); it is also associated with divine revelation and salvation—Jerome interprets 'one pearl, the most precious of all' as the knowledge of the Saviour and the sacrament of His passion and resurrection. Additionally, in Revelation 21:21, the twelve gates of the New Jerusalem are described as 'twelve pearls, each gate made of a single pearl', symbolizing purity, perfection, and access to God’s eternal presence. In Gnostic thought (e.g., Gospel of Thomas), the pearl may represent Christ or the true self, though this interpretation lies outside canonical biblical theology.

question: when did bihar bifurcate from bengal and some parts of chota nagpur merged into bengal
pred_answer: Bihar was bifurcated from Bengal in 1912, when the Bengal Presidency was reorganized and the new province of Bihar and Orissa was created — this marked Bihar’s administrative separation from Bengal. Subsequently, in 1936, Bihar and Orissa were further divided into two separate provinces: Bihar and Orissa (later Odisha). Regarding Chota Nagpur: it was part of the Bengal Presidency until 1912, then became part of Bihar and Orissa Province; after the 1936 bifurcation, Chota Nagpur Division remained within Bihar — no parts of Chota Nagpur were merged into Bengal. In fact, evidence shows movement *away* from Bengal (e.g., Cooch Behar merged *into* West Bengal in 1950; Chandannagar in 1955), but no indication that Chota Nagpur areas were merged *into* Bengal.

citations.excerpt: In 1950 the Princely State of Cooch Behar merged with West Bengal.In 1955 the former French enclave of Chandannagar, which had passed into Indian control after 1950, was integrated into West Bengal;portions of Bihar were...

citations.critique_reason: Mentions that portions of Bihar were merged with West Bengal, but does not specify when Bihar bifurcated from Bengal.

Chota Nagpur Division became part of the new province of Bihar and Orissa when it was created in 1912 to 1936.In 1936, the province was divided into two separate entities: Bihar and Orissa.Following this division, Sambalpur district was incorporated into Orissa, while Manbhum district remained in Bihar.
citations.critique_reason: The chunk mentions the division of Bihar and Orissa in 1936, but does not explicitly state when Bihar bifurcated from Bengal or when Chota Nagpur merged into Bengal.

Ruapehu erupted at 10:24 p.m. on 4 October 2006. The small eruption was marked by a magnitude 2.9 volcanic earthquake and sent waves 4–5 m (16 ft) tall crashing into the wall of the crater. No ash was erupted into the atmosphere, and the eruption is presumed to have occurred entirely underwater.

question: why was hong kong important to the british empire
pred_answer: Hong Kong was important to the British Empire primarily as a strategic free-trade zone and offshore financial centre, enabled by British legal stability, low taxes, and minimal regulation; it also served as a safe haven for political refugees during China’s 20th-century upheavals.

citations.critique_reason: Directly explains Hong Kong's economic importance to the British Empire through free trade and stability.

citations.critique_reason: Directly explains the British acquisition of Hong Kong, which answers why it was important to the British Empire.

citations.critique_reason: The chunk shows Hong Kong's role as a refuge, which relates to its importance to the British Empire, but doesn't directly address strategic or economic significance.
